chapters.title: Modern technology rewrote the economics of dating and sex
chapters.text:
Through effective contraceptives, technological progress overturned the cost-benefit math behind sex. Millions of women and men now weigh noncommittal encounters daily because consequences that once felt severe — unwanted pregnancy, sexually transmitted disease — appear manageable in ways previous generations could not imagine.

According to a 2010 Pew Research report, about forty-four percent of young adults see marriage as an obsolete institution. That figure signals a broader cultural shift away from lifelong pairing as the default intimacy script.

Birth-control technology gave women command over fertility. Many concluded premarital sex now offers more upside than downside. Intercourse carries less pregnancy risk than in prior generations when a single misstep could derail economic prospects for years. Long ago, a woman bearing a child outside marriage faced sharply reduced odds of marrying a man with solid income.

Yet even as contraceptive technology advanced, unintended pregnancies kept rising. That paradox does not mean sex tech failed; it means technology spurred social change and greater promiscuity while access, education, and inequality still mattered.

Does promiscuity pay off? Sex makes people happy, so you might expect more partners to mean more happiness. Research says otherwise: more sexual partners correlate with lower satisfaction. An economic lens explains the twentieth-century liberalization of sexual values and how widening income gaps between rich and poor fuel unintended pregnancies in impoverished communities. The chapters ahead explore each thread.

The economic approach to sexuality also helps explain why poor communities see more unintended pregnancies as income gaps widen between households.

chapters.title: Campus life produces a distinct sexual marketplace
chapters.text:
Contrary to common belief, university students have less sex on average than same-age peers who never enrolled. Campus culture still shapes outcomes in ways stereotypes miss.

On Thursday nights, students binge drink at bars near campus. Most women go for fun with friends; others use drunkenness to hook up with any willing man. Only a few seek relationships, but that path rarely works because many men expect sex within hours of meeting and will not invest in slow courtship.

Another misconception holds that men like sex more than women. Men prefer several partners; women prefer assurance sex will not be a one-off. In one study, women wanted one partner over two years on average; men wanted eight.

Male desire for multiple partners and anonymous sex fuels global sex industries.

Recently, campuses became hubs for men seeking casual sex. More women than men enroll, giving men greater sex-market power. Women know refusing first-night sex may end the connection because other women will take that risk. Women lose leverage over when and how couples first sleep together. Female abundance means less traditional dating and more hookups.

Did you know? Economist Jeffrey DeSimone found binge drinkers are twenty-five percent more likely to be sexually active, twenty percent more likely to have unprotected sex, and ninety-four percent more likely to sleep with multiple partners.

Campus bars concentrate risky behavior: drinking lowers inhibitions and raises odds of unprotected or multi-partner contact in a single evening.

chapters.title: Dating-site filters discard good matches for trivial traits
chapters.text:
Facing too many options on dating sites, people simplify via elimination — skimming entire categories to streamline choices. They reject possible partners without ever seeing them, not because of who they are but because filters excluded them. Those rejected might have been perfect fits.

Online searches push measurable traits — education, height, age, income, race — while relationship quality depends on qualities algorithms cannot score, like humor, reliability, and emotional steadiness.

Economically, narrow searches turn thick markets into artificially thin ones with weaker matches.

At the end of the market for spouses, men and women do not necessarily marry the type of person their initial preferences dictate: they marry the person who is both available and willing to be married to them. ~ Marina Adshade, PhD

To understand preferences, analyze data while markets operate. Researchers show racially segregated marriage markets driven mainly by women's same-race preference; men show less same-race sorting.

Dating markets resemble any market of buyers and sellers reaching equilibrium when both sides accept implicit prices — opportunity costs, timing, compatibility. No money changes hands, yet mispricing yourself can forfeit marriage's economic benefits.

Pricing yourself accurately speeds pairing. People overrate attractiveness, humor, intelligence, and kindness. Next login, rate your appearance honestly against same-age, same-gender profiles on the site.

Struggling to price yourself in the dating market can mean missing household economies of scale that married peers enjoy through shared expenses and specialization.

chapters.title: You marry within your market tier when options close
chapters.text:
You may not get your first choice, but someone of similar market value may be who you need. People marry for many reasons; economically, marriage is insurance in bad times plus efficient production of household goods — sex, love, children.

Marriage yields cost-effective ways to raise children safely. Childless couples still gain stable income, clean homes, and home-cooked meals. Efficient marriages split talents: a homemaking specialist pairs with a high earner, mirroring comparative advantage in trade.

Singles relocate to cities where search costs fall and match quality rises. Rural areas offer fewer encounters and more stranger skepticism.

Abhijit Banerjee and colleagues studied marriage and education in India for the American Economic Journal. Most men prefer wives less educated than themselves. Educated women enter the market older and compete with younger, less-educated women. When educated women struggle to find mates, supply exceeds demand — market disequilibrium.

If you can't find a suitable partner where you are, consider moving to a bigger city where you'll meet more people and widen the pool of plausible matches.

Comparative advantage inside marriage works like trade between nations: partners contribute different strengths so the household produces more than either could alone.

chapters.title: Marriage means different things in different societies
chapters.text:
Marriage's meaning is not fixed; it varies by place, country, and era. Communities define the institution, yet economics shapes its evolution. Heterosexual monogamy dominated Western norms, but polyamory, polyandry, and serial monogamy gain recognition today.

Respect for individual rights helped some nations accumulate wealth and advance technology. The same rights culture enabled same-sex marriage advocates to win legal recognition. When citizens reject a marital form, legal updates can institutionalize change without waiting for universal consensus.

Over the last decade, public opinion on same-sex relationships shifted rapidly. Belief evolution drives institutional change. Younger cohorts — the cohort effect — mainstream acceptance faster than older generations.

If you want to get any ideology to the mainstream, just try to make sure a high enough number of energetic young people buy into it.

Developed societies rejected polygamy because parents prefer few educated children over many less-educated ones. Monogamy supports that goal, so societies treat it as superior to polygamy.

Institutional change in marriage often follows legal reform when public opinion shifts, even before every citizen personally agrees with the new norm.

chapters.title: Similar spouses report the happiest unions
chapters.text:
Over thirty years, economic forces leveled formal labor markets for men and women, raising women's bargaining power in marriage. Spousal income gaps narrowed even as rich-poor inequality widened.

Some economists link rising divorce to closing gender earnings gaps.

Some marry to exploit gains from trade. Men importing wives from abroad try to escape domestic forces giving women marital voice. Negotiation shapes resource allocation and chores.

Technology freed women for careers and purchasable household services. Higher incomes let women leave bad marriages and stay solvent. Men refusing shared decisions risk divorce.

Many men still want wives who won't question authority. They seek partners in developing markets where women face labor disadvantages. International brokers market submissive brides; expectation clashes produce abuse and divorce.

The great marriages are partnerships. It can't be a great marriage without being a partnership. ~ Helen Mirren

Happiest couples tend to share nativity — both foreign-born or both native-born.

Gains-from-trade marriages import spouses to dodge domestic bargaining, yet clashing expectations frequently produce divorce and abuse rather than harmony.

chapters.title: People now pair off later and live outside old scripts
chapters.text:
Relationship styles evolved. More people cohabit instead of marrying early, thickening late-life romance markets.

Most people marry later wanting fewer children; marrying after thirty often means one or two kids. Fertility treatments extend childbearing; contraception and norm shifts expand premarital sex access.

People need not marry for sex. Singles keep multiple partners until ready for commitment.

Home-production technology made households more self-sufficient — women earn wages; men need not rely on women for domestic goods. Childbirth outside marriage destigmatized; teen mothers often marry much later.

Online dating and social networks cheapen mate search for older singles once isolated and costly to match.

People live longer. In 1960 women reached about eighty and men about seventy-five. Lifespans grew four to six years, encouraging longer searches for new love.

Advances in home production technology let women earn wages and purchase services once produced inside the home, reducing economic pressure to marry early.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Sex and love are not only emotion. Decisions make more sense through an economic lens. Research and financial analysis clarify preferences and actions; you choose how that shapes romantic life. Partner choice, marriage, and divorce reduce to cost analysis: what do I lose by following norms or breaking them?

It's all about the dollar. People marry equals or upward to avoid financial strain. Others avoid marriage because plentiful casual sex and career time beat commitment's price. Singleness preserves wealth-building and personal priorities.

After this summary, choose the romantic life you want — but keep dating and marriage economics in mind.

You know it's never fifty-fifty in a marriage. It's always seventy-thirty, or sixty-forty. Someone falls in love first. Someone puts someone else up on a pedestal. Someone works very hard to keep things rolling smoothly; someone else sails along for the ride. ~ Jodi Picoult

Try this

Score yourself one to ten on appearance for your age and gender. Seek partners with similar scores even if counterintuitive. If love stalls locally, relocate to a denser city.

Read it till the end?

Every romantic fork — marrying, staying single, divorcing — can be read as a cost-benefit calculation about money, time, and access to sex.
